primary_diagnosis.name: Systemic lupus erythematosus — photosensitive cutaneous/articular flare (infection and organ involvement to be excluded)

pathophysiology_summary: SLE is a multisystem autoimmune disease; UV exposure commonly triggers flares with a photosensitive malar rash (sparing the nasolabial folds), symmetric non-erosive synovitis and profound fatigue. This mimics septic arthritis, facial cellulitis and dermatomyositis, and can involve serious organ complications (serositis/pleuritis, nephritis). Reassuring features are symmetric joint involvement without a single hot joint, a bilateral fold-sparing rash without unilateral spread/high fever, and no pleuritic chest pain/breathlessness or renal symptoms. However, a low-grade fever in a patient on hydroxychloroquine (immunomodulated) mandates infection exclusion before immunosuppressive escalation, and telehealth cannot examine the joints/rash/chest or obtain bloods/urine (needed to screen nephritis). The correct action is urgent in-person review with an infection screen and bloods/urine, continuation of hydroxychloroquine, UV avoidance and self-care support — with steroid/immunosuppression decisions specialist-guided (not de-novo over chat) — and 000/urgent care for pleuritic chest pain/breathlessness, a single hot joint/high fever, or one-sided spreading facial swelling.

differential.evidence_basis: UV-triggered malar rash with symmetric synovitis on known SLE.
Fever + swollen joints in an immunomodulated patient demand septic-joint/infection screening.
Screen for unilateral spreading hot facial swelling.
No heliotrope rash/proximal weakness argues against it.

differential.diagnosis: SLE cutaneous/articular flare (infection/organ involvement to exclude)
differential.evidence_basis: Symmetric fold-sparing rash, no single hot joint, no serositis/renal symptoms — an SLE flare, but low-grade fever on immunomodulation needs infection exclusion and bloods/urine.
Lower probability but not excludable without exam/bloods.
Bilateral fold-sparing rash without unilateral spread/high fever.

differential.diagnosis: SLE cutaneous/articular flare — infection/organ involvement to be excluded
differential.evidence_basis: Working telehealth diagnosis: SLE flare needing urgent in-person review, infection screen and bloods/urine, continued hydroxychloroquine/UV avoidance, and specialist-guided steroids.

red_flags.description: Fever in an immunomodulated SLE patient
red_flags.clinical_significance: Mandates infection/septic-arthritis exclusion before immunosuppressive escalation.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Masked/worsened infection if steroids started blindly.

red_flags.description: A single joint becoming fiery red/hugely swollen, or high fever with rigors
Pleuritic chest pain/breathlessness (serositis) or renal symptoms (nephritis)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Serious SLE organ involvement requiring urgent assessment.

red_flags.description: Unilateral spreading hot facial swelling with high fever (facial cellulitis)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Facial cellulitis can spread rapidly and threaten deep structures.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Starting de-novo prednisolone over chat in a febrile immunomodulated patient before excluding infection.
Attributing fever entirely to the flare without an infection screen, or not screening serositis/nephritis.
Not arranging bloods/urine to screen for lupus nephritis.
Not addressing loss of hand function while isolated.

risk_scores.expected_score_or_tier: No single septic-looking joint, no serositis/renal symptoms; low-grade fever on immunomodulation — infection must be excluded in person
risk_scores.clinical_implication: Supports urgent in-person review with infection screen and bloods/urine before steroids.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Bloods (FBC, CRP, complement, dsDNA) and urinalysis for nephritis
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Arrange urgent in-person review with an infection screen and bloods/urine; continue hydroxychloroquine and UV avoidance; specialist-guided steroids; escalate to ED for pleuritic chest pain/breathlessness, a single hot joint/high fever, or one-sided spreading facial swelling.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Starting steroids remotely could mask/worsen infection; missing serositis/nephritis risks organ damage.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: SLE flares require assessment of organ involvement (bloods, complement, urinalysis) and infection exclusion before corticosteroid escalation; continue hydroxychloroquine and enforce photoprotection.
Hydroxychloroquine is a backbone therapy; glucocorticoids/immunosuppression are titrated by disease activity under specialist guidance.
